question: What is the worst-case time complexity of both Selection Sort and Bubble Sort?
explanation: Both Selection Sort and Bubble Sort are simple comparison-based sorting algorithms that use nested loops, resulting in a worst-case time complexity of O(n²).

question: Which of the following is a key difference between Selection Sort and Bubble Sort?
options: Selection Sort makes fewer swaps on average than Bubble Sort.
Bubble Sort is stable while Selection Sort is not.
Selection Sort is an in-place algorithm, but Bubble Sort is not.
Bubble Sort has a better worst-case time complexity.
explanation: Selection Sort makes a maximum of O(n) swaps by finding the minimum element and placing it in its correct position in each pass. Bubble Sort, in its worst case, can make O(n²) swaps as adjacent elements are swapped repeatedly.

question: After the first complete pass of a Bubble Sort algorithm on the array [5, 3, 8, 4, 2], what will the array look like?
explanation: Bubble Sort pushes the largest element to the end in the first pass. The comparisons are: (5,3)->swap[3,5,8,4,2]; (5,8)->no swap; (8,4)->swap[3,5,4,8,2]; (8,2)->swap[3,5,4,2,8]. The largest element (8) is now at the end.

question: In which scenario does Bubble Sort achieve its best-case time complexity of Ω(n), and why?
options: When the input array is already sorted.
When the input array is sorted in reverse order.
When all elements in the array are identical.
When the array contains many duplicate elements.
explanation: An optimized version of Bubble Sort (with a flag to check if any swaps occurred) can detect that the array is already sorted after a single pass. Since no swaps are made in that pass, the algorithm terminates, resulting in only n-1 comparisons and a best-case complexity of Ω(n).

question: Consider sorting an array where each element is at most k positions away from its sorted location. How can the performance of Selection Sort be analyzed in this specific context?
options: Its time complexity remains O(n²) as the algorithm doesn't exploit the partial order.
Its time complexity improves to O(n log n) due to the reduced number of comparisons.
Its time complexity becomes linear, O(kn), as it only needs k passes.
It becomes unstable, but its time complexity improves to O(nk).
explanation: Selection Sort has a fixed structure. It always performs n(n-1)/2 comparisons, regardless of the initial order of the input. Therefore, even for a partially sorted array, its time complexity remains O(n²), as it does not have a mechanism to exploit the existing partial order.
